text.title: Text-to-Speech — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules: latency-bucket provider pick, voice cloning consent, cache-by-text-hash, streaming for real-time UX, mandatory fallback provider.

rule.statement: Provider MUST be picked by use-case latency bucket: real-time (≤300ms TTFB) → Deepgram Aura / ElevenLabs Turbo; near-real-time (300-800ms) → OpenAI tts-1 / Google Chirp; batch → Coqui local or ElevenLabs multilingual.
rule.rationale: Latency varies 4× between providers. Picking by latency bucket prevents shipping tts-1 into a real-time agent that needs ≤300ms.

rule.statement: Voice cloning (custom voice trained on a specific person) MUST have a signed consent record per voice. The TTS call MUST refuse a clone voice ID lacking consent_recorded=true in the catalog.

rationale.source: ethics + IP risk; EU AI Act provisions

rule.rationale: Cloning a real person's voice without consent is fraud / impersonation in many jurisdictions and a EU AI Act high-risk obligation. Enforce at config layer; do not rely on operator memory.

rule.statement: Production TTS MUST cache audio output keyed by sha256(text + voice_id + model + version). Cache TTL ≥ 7 days. Skipping cache wastes 40-70% of inference cost.
rule.rationale: Identical phrases recur ("How can I help you?", "Please hold"). Hash-by-text cache nets 40-70% hit rate in real traffic.

rule.statement: Real-time use cases (voice agents, IVR) MUST use streaming TTS (provider returns audio chunks as it generates). Buffering full audio before play is forbidden for real-time.
rule.rationale: Buffering full audio adds the full synthesis duration to perceived latency. Streaming reduces TTFB to ≈200ms regardless of total length.

rule.statement: Production deployment MUST declare a fallback provider != primary. Primary outage triggers fallback within 1s.
rule.rationale: TTS providers have outages. Hard-coded single provider takes voice features down for the duration. Fallback to a different provider OR local Coqui keeps voice flow alive.
